title: Neural tract tracing using Di-I: a review and a new method to make fast Di-I faster in human brain
abstract: The use of Di-I in tract-tracing is briefly reviewed and a novel delayed-fixation approach to neural tract-tracing in the postmortem human adult brain is reported. Using the new approach, fast Di-I, a highly lipophilic fluorescent dye was injected into a particular region or nucleus and labelled tracts were followed for distances of some 20-40 mm. The procedure required approximately 36 h, yielding dye penetration rates of 1.0 mm/h or more. This contrasts with previous Di-I, silver impregnation, and horseradish peroxidase protocols, where the tracer penetration rate is typically 0.003 mm/h or less. The new method hinges on the simple consideration that aldehyde fixation, which is normally employed prior to administration of the marker, crosslinks membrane proteins and impedes dye diffusion. Using these methods we confirmed the presence of an ipsilateral olivocerebellar climbing fiber projection in the human.

title: Oh et al. - A mesoscale connectome of the mouse brain
abstract: We describe a mesoscale connectome of the mouse brain, a comprehensive map of long-range connectivity based on 469 anterograde viral tract-tracing experiments covering 295 regions. Connectivity data are described by a weighted, directed graph with nodes corresponding to brain regions and edges reflecting projections. Regional and cell-type-specific projections were mapped using recombinant AAV expressing EGFP. Injections covered the whole brain and terminal fields of projections were systematically mapped using serial two-photon tomography. The resulting whole-brain connectivity matrix reveals topographical organization and provides insight into hierarchical structure of the mouse brain connectome.

title: Basser et al. - Diffusion tensor MRI: theory and application
abstract: Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI) was developed to measure the directional dependence of water diffusion in tissues. DTI enables the visualization of white matter fasciculi in the living human brain. The principal eigenvector of the diffusion tensor is used to estimate the local fiber orientation, from which tractography algorithms follow fiber paths through the white matter. DTI provides a powerful noninvasive method for studying white matter integrity and connectivity. However, DTI cannot differentiate the directionality of signal transmission along fiber tracts, only their physical orientation. The method suffers from limitations including the partial volume effect and inability to resolve crossing fibers in a single voxel.

title: Massimini et al. - Breakdown of cortical effective connectivity during sleep
abstract: We directly probed the causal interactions between cortical areas in sleeping and waking humans by combining TMS with EEG. In waking, a TMS pulse triggered a complex pattern of activations traveling to connected areas. This cortical effective connectivity, measured as the propagation of TMS-evoked potentials, was systematically reduced during sleep. The TMS-EEG approach provides a direct measure of directed effective connectivity by stimulating one region and recording the causal downstream response in connected regions via high-density EEG.

title: Matsumoto et al. - Corticocortical evoked potentials (CCEP) in patients with epilepsy
abstract: Corticocortical evoked potentials were recorded via intracranial electrodes in epilepsy patients undergoing presurgical evaluation. Single pulse electrical stimulation was applied to one electrode site while responses were recorded from all other sites. The resulting CCEPs provide a direct measure of monosynaptic and early polysynaptic effective connectivity between stimulated and recording sites. The direction of connectivity is unambiguously defined by the stimulation-recording relationship. This method is applicable only to patients with implanted SEEG or subdural electrodes.
